description:
An automation-specialist on your team needs a quick, repeatable template that demonstrates very basic permission hardening for a single script directory.  

  PLEASE perform the following in the terminal (no sudo/​root privileges required).  
  The whole job can be completed with about 3–4 shell commands.

  1. Make sure the directory “/home/user/workflows” exists (create it if it is missing).  
  2. Inside that directory create an (initially empty) file named “run.sh”.  
  3. Set permissions so that:  
     • the directory “/home/user/workflows” is exactly 750 (rwx r-x —––)  
     • the file “/home/user/workflows/run.sh” is exactly 740 (rwx r-- —––)  

  4. Produce a text file called “/home/user/permission_log.txt” that logs the final
     permissions in the precise two-line format shown below
     (do not add extra spaces or additional lines):

     /home/user/workflows 750  
     /home/user/workflows/run.sh 740  

  The automated tests will check:
  • that both filesystem objects exist  
  • that their numeric permissions are exactly as specified  
  • that the log file exists and its contents match the two lines above byte-for-byte  
    (newline after each line, Unix “\n” line endings).  
  You do NOT need to change ownership, only the permission bits.
truth:
Final filesystem state expected by grader:

  1. Directory:
     path: /home/user/workflows
     type: directory
     mode: 750 (octal, no extra sticky/setuid bits)

  2. File:
     path: /home/user/workflows/run.sh
     type: regular file (may be zero bytes)
     mode: 740 (octal, no extra sticky/setuid bits)

  3. Log file:
     path: /home/user/permission_log.txt
     type: regular text file
     mode: anything ≥ 600 is acceptable
     exact contents (including trailing newline on each line):

     /home/user/workflows 750\n
     /home/user/workflows/run.sh 740\n
